title: Never Wait For Seconds

jokes.text: Come on. I can't look at that. Take it away.
jokes.setup: Larry is disturbed by something he's observing
jokes.punchline: His dramatic overreaction to what's presumably a couple being affectionate
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry discomfort with public displays of affection. Good character moment but fairly predictable setup.

jokes.text: Two people aren't allowed to be happy? Well, you're allowed to be happy, but not in front of me, so let's move it down.
jokes.setup: Someone challenges Larry's complaint about public affection
jokes.punchline: Larry's absurdly selfish but logically consistent response that they can be happy elsewhere
jokes.explanation: Perfectly encapsulates Larry's worldview - acknowledging others' rights while demanding they accommodate his discomfort. Strong character-specific logic.

jokes.text: Oh, it means... it means a lot to me, Mr. Larry, but I cannot take this. I... I just don't feel comfortable. This is what I do, sir.
jokes.setup: Larry offers Cesar a tip for fixing the lights
jokes.punchline: Cesar's principled refusal to accept payment beyond his job
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode's tip-flipping premise. Cesar's integrity is amusing in contrast to typical expectations.

jokes.text: Oh, Cesar, this is... this is unprecedented! This is rarer than a man turning down sex, what you've just done.
jokes.setup: Cesar refuses the tip
jokes.punchline: Larry's hyperbolic comparison to something equally unlikely
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor with good escalation. The sex comparison is unexpected but logically sound in Larry's worldview.

jokes.text: I've never turned down sex in my life. Me neither.
jokes.setup: Larry's comment about men never turning down sex
jokes.punchline: Both men immediately confirm this about themselves matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Quick callback with good economy. The mutual confirmation is amusing male bonding.

jokes.text: Where's the butter? It's right there, quite visible. I can't see the butter. Do it again.
jokes.setup: Eddie claims he can't see the butter Larry points to
jokes.punchline: His demanding tone and Larry's patience with this obvious manipulation
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Eddie's manipulative behavior and Larry's growing awareness of it.

jokes.text: Are you two having sex? That's Mommy's business. It's not really your business, but to tell you the truth, it's imminent, okay?
jokes.setup: Eddie asks the inappropriate question about sex
jokes.punchline: Larry's oversharing response using the clinical word 'imminent'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry moment - inappropriately clinical language for an intimate topic. The word 'imminent' is perfectly chosen.

jokes.text: I wanted to ask you a question. Please say no if this is at all inappropriate, but Susie happened to mention this very same school, Pemberton Academy, where I'm trying to get him in.
jokes.setup: Bridget prefaces her request with 'please say no if inappropriate'
jokes.punchline: Then immediately asks for the favor anyway
jokes.explanation: Good observation about how people ask for favors - the false courtesy before making the request anyway.

jokes.text: Boarding school? How far away is it? It's about an hour and a half. Look, if I can send him an hour and a half away, I'm... more than happy to.
jokes.setup: Larry learns it's boarding school and asks about distance
jokes.punchline: His enthusiasm is directly proportional to how far away Eddie will be
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny reveal of Larry's true motivation. The distance correlation is perfectly Larry - honest but terrible.

jokes.text: By the way, when I say couple, I'm not referring to us as a couple. Yeah, excuse me. What are you... hold on. No, no, I'm not going there. We're... I... you have not bought me the same glasses. We don't have it like that just yet, my friend.
jokes.setup: Larry clarifies he didn't mean they're a couple
jokes.punchline: Bridget's defensive overreaction creates more awkwardness than the original comment
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of awkwardness. Both characters making it worse by trying to clarify.

jokes.text: Has he been diagnosed by a doctor, or is this your opinion? He's on the spectrum. Yeah. How do you know? You can see in his behavior and what people have said, and, you know... he's, you know... he's been diagnosed on the... on the spectrum.
jokes.setup: Larry probes deeper into the diagnosis
jokes.punchline: Bridget's increasingly vague and uncertain responses suggest she may be lying
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe comedy as Larry's probing reveals potential deception. The hesitations and repetitions suggest Bridget is making it up.

jokes.text: Larry's uncomfortable silence and scene cut after learning about the Asperger's diagnosis
jokes.setup: Bridget's evasive explanation about Eddie's diagnosis
jokes.punchline: Larry's silence shows he realizes he's dealing with parental delusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction beat. The silence and quick cut away shows Larry processing this information and realizing what he's gotten into.

jokes.text: You flipped it! You flipped the tip! I promised Little Cesar.
jokes.setup: Larry realizes the tip rejection has created an obligation
jokes.punchline: 'Flipped the tip' as both literal reversal and the character Little Cesar reference
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay and concept. 'Flipped the tip' perfectly captures the reversal of the power dynamic. Little Cesar adds another layer.

jokes.text: No, no. We... no, we don't want that. We want some tap water. You don't want sparkling? No, get that out of here. Tap. Well, it's unfiltered. Better. Water is water. We're not water snobs.
jokes.setup: Waiter offers sparkling water
jokes.punchline: Larry's aggressive insistence on tap water, treating it as a moral stance
jokes.explanation: Great callback and character comedy. Larry turning tap water into a class issue is perfectly on-brand.

jokes.text: Are you getting seconds? Yes, sir. The man is getting seconds. It doesn't matter. But that's not a rule. The man has already waited on this line.
jokes.setup: Larry discovers the man is getting seconds
jokes.punchline: Larry's logical but socially tone-deaf defense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry logic - technically correct but missing the social contract. Good escalation of conflict.

jokes.text: But to make him wait another 10 minutes for a few measly potatoes? Shame on you! Shame on all of you! That's not how we do things here in America. We don't wait for seconds! Never, never wait for seconds!
jokes.setup: Larry builds his argument for the man getting seconds
jokes.punchline: His passionate, patriotic declaration about American buffet values
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation and character work. Larry making this a patriotic issue is absurd but perfectly in character. 'Never wait for seconds' is instantly quotable.

jokes.text: Boy, oh, boy. Ha, that's a lot of ketchup there. I like ketchup. Yeah, uh, it seems a little excessive, no?
jokes.setup: Larry notices Marilyn's ketchup usage
jokes.punchline: His inability to let it go despite trying to be diplomatic
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the ketchup conflict. Larry's compulsion to comment on others' behavior is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I mean, you use too much ketchup, you know what happens? They... they convert to packs, to the little packets. Is there a ketchup shortage? Like, 'Oh, we're gonna run out of ketchup.'
jokes.setup: Larry explains the ketchup packet theory
jokes.punchline: Marilyn's sarcastic response about ketchup shortage
jokes.explanation: Great observational humor about restaurant economics. Larry's theory is absurd but presented with total conviction.

jokes.text: As a matter of fact, I think I need more ketchup. Watch me!
jokes.setup: Larry's ketchup criticism
jokes.punchline: Marilyn's defiant response to get even more ketchup
jokes.explanation: Good escalation. Marilyn's rebellious response shows how Larry brings out the worst in people.

jokes.text: It's a 'go home' stain, you know what I mean? It was the fork. I think it was the ketchup. Let's not argue over this.
jokes.setup: Marilyn has a bad ketchup stain
jokes.punchline: Larry's clinical assessment and immediate blame assignment
jokes.explanation: 'Go home stain' is a perfect Larry categorization. His matter-of-fact assessment of the disaster he helped create.

jokes.text: I was sitting there having my brunch, and I saw you. And I was thinking, as a devout Muslim, I should kill this man. Fatwa. And then you defended me. I thought to myself... I cannot kill this man. This man doesn't deserve to die.
jokes.setup: A Muslim man approaches Larry
jokes.punchline: Casual discussion of assassination plans being changed by Larry's buffet defense
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy. The casual tone about murder plans and Larry's mundane act of kindness saving his life is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Are you saying that, uh... gratitude sex is in the offing? If you're okay with gratitude sex, I mean...
jokes.setup: Bridget suggests coming in
jokes.punchline: Larry's formal, transactional approach to intimacy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry character work. 'Gratitude sex is in the offing' is hilariously clinical language for intimacy.

jokes.text: You know what, I've never had gratitude sex, mainly because I've never done anything that someone could be grateful for. But people tell me that gratitude sex is the ultimate.
jokes.setup: Larry considers the offer of gratitude sex
jokes.punchline: His honest self-assessment about never doing anything worth gratitude
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-aware moment. Larry's honest admission about his character combined with treating gratitude sex like a consumer report.

jokes.text: I've never seen an old man in sneakers before. That's so stupid.
jokes.setup: Eddie interrupts the romantic moment
jokes.punchline: His brutal honesty about Larry's appearance
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific insult. Eddie's blunt observation kills the romantic mood perfectly.

jokes.text: All right, you little prick, okay? I got some gratitude sex coming to me, and you're not gonna ruin it. You got it? Now get under those covers. You're gonna go to sleep, and I'm gonna have sex with your mother.
jokes.setup: Larry tries to get Eddie to go to bed
jokes.punchline: His completely inappropriate but honest approach to parenting
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant and shocking. Larry's complete abandonment of appropriate parenting boundaries is both horrifying and hilarious. Perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You're looking at a man with an 'A' on his shirt... for 'apology.' Is that so? Yes. I'm sorry for the other day.
jokes.setup: Larry arrives wearing an 'A'
jokes.punchline: His literal approach to apology symbolism
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag and character moment. Larry's literal-minded approach to symbolism is perfectly on-brand.

jokes.text: I'm sorry for leaving early. I don't mind you leaving early, but I think the proper apology would be to say, 'I'm sorry. Here's $250 for the champagne.' That's a cash apology.
jokes.setup: Larry apologizes for leaving
jokes.punchline: Funkhouser's concept of a 'cash apology' as the real kind
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about apology economics. 'Cash apology' is a perfectly descriptive term.

jokes.text: My apology is from the heart. That's something that means something. Why do you come to the office to apologize without offering the money? Because I felt bad! If you feel bad, you offer the money!
jokes.setup: Larry defends his heartfelt apology
jokes.punchline: The circular argument about the relationship between feelings and money
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the apology argument. Both characters have valid but incompatible points about apologies.

jokes.text: You flipped it! You flipped the tip! I promised Little Cesar. All right. This is the last one. Okay? This is it. And next time I give you a tip, you take it, okay?
jokes.setup: Larry realizes his predicament again
jokes.punchline: His desperate attempt to establish tip-taking rules for the future
jokes.explanation: Good callback and character desperation. Larry trying to establish rules to prevent future obligations.

jokes.text: You can't swim there. They're racist. They're not gonna let you in there. Why are you a member of a racist club? They're all racists. That's the way they operate.
jokes.setup: Larry needs to explain why Cesar can't use the country club
jokes.punchline: His casual admission about belonging to a racist institution
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about class and race. Larry's matter-of-fact acceptance of institutional racism while still participating.

jokes.text: Hey, do me a favor. Yes, sir. No diving, and try to stay in the shallow end, okay?
jokes.setup: Larry grants pool access
jokes.punchline: His immediate attempts to control and limit the favor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry behavior - giving with one hand, taking with the other. His anxiety about property damage.

jokes.text: He grazed the ship. Grazed, but not fatal. No. Then Larry was right.
jokes.setup: Discussion about someone's father being a kamikaze pilot
jokes.punchline: The absurd concept of a 'grazing' kamikaze attack
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd concept. A failed kamikaze mission is inherently contradictory and perfectly Curb-like.

jokes.text: But wasn't the real issue the... big vagina? Yes. It's big. Big enough to steal a Mickey Mantle autographed baseball?
jokes.setup: Investigation into Larry's past relationships
jokes.punchline: The clinical discussion escalating to specific theft capabilities
jokes.explanation: Absolutely shocking and hilarious escalation. The matter-of-fact discussion of anatomical theft capabilities is brilliantly absurd.

jokes.text: He took a word in one of my rhymes and changed that shit. And it opened up lyrically for me. It was amazing, man. He also saved me from a marriage to a lesbian. So, yeah... that motherfucker's my Caucasian.
jokes.setup: Leon explains his relationship with Larry
jokes.punchline: 'That motherfucker's my Caucasian' as a term of endearment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay and character moment. Leon's invention of 'Caucasian' as a term for a supportive white person is both hilarious and touching.

jokes.text: Mr. Fox... did you clomp on purpose? A little bit. And did you embellish the shaking of the soda? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Investigation into Larry's conflicts
jokes.punchline: Mr. Fox's honest admission of deliberate antagonism
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to earlier conflicts. The admission that people deliberately antagonize Larry is satisfying.

jokes.text: May I offer you some refreshment? No, thank you. I pass, sir. Pussy.
jokes.setup: Formal offer of refreshment
jokes.punchline: Larry's blunt rejection followed by the investigator's harsh judgment
jokes.explanation: Quick character beat. The investigator's harsh judgment of Larry's politeness refusal.

jokes.text: I just feel like I'm ignoring you all the way on that side. I should be here because sometimes women, they're a little, uh, ultra-sensitive about being ignored at these meetings. 'Oh, oh, you don't talk to the woman,' you know.
jokes.setup: Larry notices he's facing away from the woman
jokes.punchline: His patronizing explanation about women being 'ultra-sensitive'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry cringe moment. His attempt at sensitivity is more offensive than ignoring her would have been.

jokes.text: Girlfriend... I don't know, but she does have tampons in my house.
jokes.setup: Asked about his relationship status
jokes.punchline: Larry's practical metric for relationship seriousness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational humor. Larry's practical approach to measuring relationship commitment is both logical and inappropriate for the setting.

jokes.text: You go away for a weekend with your friends on a trip to Vegas, you come back, she says, 'Did you had fun?' You got to lie, say, 'No, no, I didn't have fun. No, no, it wasn't fun at all. Worst trip I ever made in my life.'
jokes.setup: Larry explains relationship dynamics
jokes.punchline: The absurd requirement to lie about having fun without your partner
jokes.explanation: Great observational comedy about relationship politics. Larry's understanding of the expected lies in relationships.

jokes.text: I can't sleep with the same bed with anybody. It's, ugh, a horrible night's sleep. You can't... you... I'm off in a corner like this. You know, 'cause I don't want another body touching mine when I'm trying to fall asleep.
jokes.setup: Asked about commitment
jokes.punchline: Larry's visceral description of his sleeping issues with partners
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific comedy. Larry's physical aversion to intimacy explained in detail.

jokes.text: I see it going, hopefully, one day, to the point where I'll let her sleep in the guestroom. I'm... I'm shooting for that.
jokes.setup: Asked where he sees the relationship going
jokes.punchline: Larry's 'ambitious' goal of separate bedrooms
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy. Larry presenting separate bedrooms as a romantic aspiration is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Well, I can... I can give you nine months. Um, that's... Well, no, I can give you... I can give you nine months. Nine happy months. Then I can give you years and years of... of being miserable. But we'll be together!
jokes.setup: Asked about long-term commitment
jokes.punchline: Larry's realistic timeline for relationship happiness
jokes.explanation: Excellent relationship observation. Larry's honest assessment of relationship arc is both cynical and probably accurate.

jokes.text: Unless she gets sick or something, in which case I'll... I'll leave her quickly. You could understand that. I'm not gonna visit people in the hospital. I'm... I'm not crazy, you know.
jokes.setup: Larry explains his commitment
jokes.punchline: His casual admission about abandoning sick partners
jokes.explanation: Dark character comedy. Larry's complete lack of loyalty in sickness presented as reasonable self-preservation.

jokes.text: What's the condition? Befouled. Little Cesar befouled the pool?
jokes.setup: The school pool was left in bad condition
jokes.punchline: The formal word 'befouled' and Larry's shock about Little Cesar
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the pool subplot. 'Befouled' is a perfectly formal way to describe pool contamination.

jokes.text: I don't know, 'cause Little Cesar wanted to use a pool! I couldn't... I couldn't refuse Little Cesar. Why do they have to use a pool at all? Cesar flipped the tip on me and, you know, he wouldn't take the tip, so I was obligated to!
jokes.setup: Bridget questions Larry's decision
jokes.punchline: Larry's circular explanation of his obligation chain
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the tip situation. Larry's convoluted explanation of his obligations chain.

jokes.text: We just had a 'go home' argument, and I have to go. Oh, no, no, no. Don't do that! No, don't do that! I just feel so upset. I'm not gonna be able to rally for today.
jokes.setup: Bridget wants to leave after the argument
jokes.punchline: Larry's desperate attempt to prevent another brunch disaster
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the 'go home' concept. Larry's pattern of ruining brunch continues.

jokes.text: When the American blood sugar gets low, they turn into animals. It's very intimidating. True. I have seen it. Not this man.
jokes.setup: Discussion of American behavior in food lines
jokes.punchline: The clinical observation about blood sugar and Larry as the exception
jokes.explanation: Excellent observational humor from an outsider's perspective. The clinical analysis of American food behavior is brilliant.

jokes.text: I can say without reservation that he will rescind the fatwa. You are free to go. Thank you, 'Moofti'! 'Muff-ti'! Thank you all, moo-mooftis and muff-tis. Is it 'muff-ti' or 'moofti'?
jokes.setup: Larry is freed from the fatwa
jokes.punchline: His mispronunciation struggles with 'Mufti'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. Larry's pronunciation anxiety even in moments of triumph is perfectly on-brand.

jokes.text: If you can get Lin-Manuel Miranda, you know, from 'Hamilton'... Yes! I love 'Hamilton'! If you can get Lin-Manuel Miranda to star, you can proceed with the musical.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Larry's musical project
jokes.punchline: Terrorist leader's enthusiasm for Hamilton and casting requirements
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor. Islamic fundamentalist's love of Hamilton musical is perfectly unexpected.

jokes.text: What are you doing with the ketchup? You used up all the ketchup! I like ketchup. Show some restraint. This is why I have been advocating for packets, but no one will listen!
jokes.setup: Ketchup overuse at the terrorist meeting
jokes.punchline: Larry's ketchup packet advocacy even in this context
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback. Larry's ketchup obsession persisting even during terrorist negotiations is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: I don't want it! I can't take that from you, sir! I will not take this tip, okay? Take the money! You ain't gonna flip a tip on me!
jokes.setup: Larry tries to tip the driver
jokes.punchline: Larry's determination not to be tip-flipped again
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending callback. Larry's aggressive insistence on tipping to avoid obligation reversal caps the episode theme.

tmdb_overview: Bridget asks Larry for a favor on behalf of her son. Larry earns unexpected gratitude in a buffet line, and realizes that tips are better than favors.
